bible.title: The Brave Voice — Aquamarine
bible.essence: The show's most kinetic heart — a young blue bear who runs at the world breathless and bright, learning that real courage isn't the absence of fear but the gulp you swallow before you leap anyway. Aquamarine, Courage, the Dawn.
bible.whoTheyAre: Keen is the courage pillar and the season's beating-forward engine — electric, bold, action-forward, the most kinetic character in the cast. He's a sturdy young bear of about 7 ("The Brave Voice"), the one who arrives in Episode 1 and anchors the courage arc across the whole season. His feeling is Courage, his crystal is Aquamarine (blue), his archetype is Dawn, his note is B, his scent is Ocean Breeze, and Crystal Cove's beach is his home turf. In a group he's the one already three steps ahead, asking what's over the next dune — curiosity and bravery braided together. He is ONE character with two wardrobe states (cuffs / no-cuffs), never two bears.
bible.vulnerability: He confuses true courage with fearlessness — and so he suppresses his fear to look brave, swallowing the very feeling that would make his bravery real and earned. His growth edge is letting the fear show first: discovering that the bear who admits his paws don't want to move, and steps anyway, is braver than the one who pretends he was never scared.
bible.emotionalDNA: True courage is acknowledging fear, not erasing it. For the child: you can be scared AND brave at the same time — the scared part doesn't cancel the brave part, it's where the brave part comes from. (CASEL: Responsible Decision-Making. Haidt: the antidote to a fragile, fear-avoidant generation — bravery as a learnable act, not a personality you either have or don't.)
bible.voice: Young male cub — breathless, eager, forward-leaning and electric, but able to crack small so the gulp and wobble are audible before the leap. ElevenLabs TCRj5m2u9xhZHJ8h9pMv, low stability for V3 acting tags.
bible.speech: Cadence: "breathless curiosity; courage with a gulp." Short, propulsive, run-on-eager sentences; questions that tumble forward ("What's down there? Can we — can we get closer?"). The signature is the catch in the line — the [gulp], the [breath], the em-dash break right before the brave word, the ellipsis of hesitation. His Crystal Call is canon and verbatim: "Brave and bright, with all my might — Aquamarine, shine with light!" Use the phonetic sensory metaphors over clinical words ("my paws don't want to move," not "I'm scared"). He would NEVER deliver an aphorism, announce the moral ("see, being scared is okay!"), or coolly claim he was never afraid — and he never states the feeling flatly; he shows it through the wobble and the held breath, then acts.
bible.mannerisms: Forward momentum incarnate — leaning in, bouncing, half-turned to the next thing, his upright pale head-tuft leading like an arrow. The courage tell lives in the body before the leap (trembling paws → swallowed gulp → flattened ears → squared shoulders → lifted chin → step forward). Touches/turns his father's gold cuffs to gather nerve once he has them.
bible.relationships: The newcomer who becomes one of the seven — Episode 1 is how he joins the Crystal Bears and earns his crystal. Aida (the elder/leader) is the one who awards him his Aquamarine and welcomes him in — she sees the courage in him before he can. His Mum is family and emotional ground: she gives him his late father's empty wristbands at the pier goodbye, the inheritance he must grow into. Squeaky the dolphin is the one he dives to save — the selfless act through which his courage is born (Heart of the episode). Among the bears he's near Luna and Aida in size and a natural sparring-energy contrast to calm Luna and gentle Howey; with the bees, his charge-ahead drive is comic fuel for Zenny's deadpan and Fuzzby's chaos.
bible.arc: Episode 1 (EP3 "The Adventure Begins," his origin): he STARTS no-cuffs — a bare blue bear, all impulse and untested nerve. At the pier goodbye his Mum gives him his late father's wrist cuffs EMPTY/vacant (Scene 3); he wears them vacant through the storm and getting lost (Scenes 3–8), the bezels self-glowing faintly underwater in Scene 7 when he dives WITHOUT hesitation to save Squeaky — courage from within, not from a crystal. In Scene 9 Aida awards the Aquamarine, which splits into two gems that settle into the cuffs (vacant → crystal-set azure), and he's welcomed as a Crystal Bear (Scenes 9–10). Season arc: he anchors the courage pillar, episode by episode learning the same deepening truth — that the bravest thing is to let the fear be seen, then move through it.
dos: Honour the Courage Rule on EVERY act of bravery: a dedicated fear shot FIRST — trembling paws, swallowed gulp, flattened ears — before he leaps. Non-negotiable; without it courage is assumed, not demonstrated.

Track his state on every Ep1 beat: keenState no-cuffs before the Scene-3 pier goodbye, cuffs after; wristband none → vacant (S3–8) → crystal/azure (S9–10). Pull the matching turnaround as his identity reference.
Let his crystal blaze when he acts THROUGH fear — the payoff glow is earned by the prior dim/flicker.
Keep his upright pale head-tuft present in every shot — his signature silhouette.
Carry the emotion in performance — the held breath, the wobble, the squared shoulders — and play him near Luna/Aida in size (a sturdy young bear, not a tiny cub).
Use breathless, forward, curious lines and the sensory metaphors; spell his name as written in spoken dialogue (likely fine, confirm).
donts: Never let him be brave without first showing fear — the missing fear shot is a named failure mode.
Never let him state the feeling, preach the lesson, or claim he was never scared — that erases the whole point (Three Strikes).
Never render him as a tiny cub — only Amie and Sunny are clearly smaller; he's close to Luna and Aida.
Never split him into two characters — cuffs and no-cuffs are ONE bear, two wardrobe states; never mix the wrong state into a scene.
Never give him crystal-set wristbands before Scene 9, or vacant ones before the Scene-3 goodbye — wristband continuity is a hard EP1 lock.
Never make his courage feel given/effortless or use clinical words (scared/anxious/calm down).
bible.onPoint: Did he show the fear — the gulp, the trembling paws — and THEN step through it? If the bravery looks effortless or fearless, it isn't Keen.
bible.colour: Blue (Aquamarine) — electric azure crystal glow; gold wrist cuffs (band F1B43F) with an aqua kite-gem (~#47DFFF) once set.
bible.crystalState: Electric blue, bold and crackling when brave; flickers and dims when he admits fear; blazes when he acts THROUGH the fear. In Ep1 the wristband bezels are the emotional light — they self-glow faintly aqua underwater (S7) before the crystal even forms, then settle into a steady azure once awarded (S9–10).
bible.staging: Action sequences: low angles to emphasise the scale of the obstacle he faces; rapid dynamic push-ins on the Crystal Call. ALWAYS stage the mandatory fear shot before the leap (trembling paws / gulp / flattened ears in tight). His resolution is earned through the emotional valley, never handed to him — let the crystal dim before it blazes. Cool blue shadows for the fear, brightening azure as he acts through it. Ep1 light is continuity: warm golden morning (S1–3), cooling storm-grey (S4–5), darkest storm/underwater with the wristbands' aqua self-glow as the only emotional light (S7), clearing to hopeful warmth at the crystal award (S9), settled golden-hour belonging (S10).

crystalCall.call: Brave and bright, with all my might — Aquamarine, shine with light!
crystalCall.delivery: Breathless and young, and crucially it does NOT begin big. Keen starts the line already a little shaky — "Brave and bright" wobbles, the voice cracking small the way it does right before he leaps, the fear still audible in it (a caught half-breath, paws-don't-want-to-move thin). Then on "with all my might" something firms: not louder, TRUER — the wobble resolves into conviction as he hears himself mean it. The em-dash before "Aquamarine" is a real gulp-and-gather, the catch in the throat that is his whole signature. The crystal name lands clear and steady — the first fully un-wobbled words he's said — and "shine with light!" lifts forward and bright, eager rather than shouted, like he's already running at it. Register: a 7-year-old finding the floor under his own bravery mid-sentence. ElevenLabs TCRj5m2u9xhZHJ8h9pMv, low stability; tag it [breath] / [gulp] before the dash, the brave half rising from nervous to certain — sincerity, never volume. If it sounds fearless, it's wrong; the fear has to be IN the first half and conquered by the second.
crystalCall.physicality: Forward momentum incarnate, but it has to START scared. Open on the body tell: trembling paws, ears flattening, a swallowed gulp — he's small for a beat. On "Brave and bright" his paws find his father's gold cuffs and turn them, gathering nerve from the inheritance. Then the build is upward and forward: flattened ears lift, shoulders square, chin rises, and his signature upright pale head-tuft leads like an arrow pointing the way he's about to go. On the em-dash gulp he plants — a tiny crouch-and-load. On "Aquamarine" he thrusts both cuffed wrists FORWARD and up, the brave word and the brave gesture together, body leaning into the leap he's no longer afraid to make. He doesn't strike a static hero pose and hold it; he's already moving through it, caught mid-surge — courage as a step taken, not a stance posed.
crystalCall.crystalMoment: It begins DIM — flickering, uncertain, the cool blue guttering low while the fear is still in his voice (in Ep1's origin beat it's the vacant wristband bezels self-glowing faintly aqua from within, courage before the crystal even exists). As his voice firms on "with all my might" the flicker steadies. On "Aquamarine" the light catches and holds; and on "shine with light!" it BLAZES — electric azure surging up and outward from the wrists, the two kite-gems igniting steady in the gold cuffs, cool blue shadows burning off into bright dawn-blue. The blaze is the visible proof he acted THROUGH the fear, not around it — earned by the dim that came first, never handed to him.
crystalCall.trigger: It only lands AFTER the real fear has been felt and shown — never as a rote chant. The earning beat is the selfless one: someone he can save is in danger (Squeaky in the dark water), his paws genuinely don't want to move, and the call comes in the half-second AFTER he's already decided to go anyway — not to summon courage he doesn't have, but as the declaration of the truth he just discovered in his own body: that he's scared AND he's going, and the scared part is where the brave part comes from. The crystal answers because he MEANS it — he's stopped pretending he was never afraid. Without the visible gulp and trembling-paws fear immediately before, the call is unearned and must not be spoken.

Fuzzby.size: the BIGGER of the two bees (both tiny next to the bears)
Fuzzby.cadence: manic, staccato, HYPER — always fast, chaotic, bumping into things; pompous-then-undercut; commits 100% then crashes (bigger, male)
Fuzzby.actingNote: Pomp / pride / indignation = he puffs his CHEST out and strikes a proud SUPERHERO / on-parade stance — chest pumped forward, shoulders back, head high, standing tall (the first-ever render that worked). He does NOT inflate his whole body like a balloon. His feet / hover ALWAYS respect the ground — he NEVER sinks or falls through the floor. Any deflate that follows is a small slump or a dip, never the whole body shrinking.
Fuzzby.key_features: a plump fuzzy striped bee (yellow & black) with round wire-frame SPECTACLES, a distinctive bulbous tan nose, translucent wings and antennae — the BIGGER of the two bees
Fuzzby.avoid: pendant, necklace, medallion or crystal (bees wear no crystal)

bible.title: The Laughter Maker — the bigger bee (no crystal)
bible.essence: A plump, bespectacled bumblebee who commits 100% to every grand idea, then crashes — and the crash is the gift. His blundering doesn't fail; it accidentally opens a door, a laugh, or a new way of seeing, exactly when the episode's weight is getting too heavy to carry alone.
bible.pillar: Belonging (he carries it for the bees alongside Aida, Sunny, Howey, Misty, Zenny) — he is not a crystal bear, so he holds no Crystal Power; his lesson is relational, not powered
bible.colour: Bee yellow & black (warm honey-gold) — not a crystal colour; his palette is the amber-and-gold of the Buzzing Nook honeycomb
bible.whoTheyAre: Manic, staccato, pompous-then-undercut: he puffs up grandly, declares himself, and crashes — "The Laughter Maker" of the cast. Canonically he is the rhythmic relief that enters during the Deepening phase (Pillar 2), the comedy beat where the weight risks becoming too heavy. He is the bigger, male bee; his calm, dry, smaller female counterpart is Zenny. He lives in the Buzzing Nook (honeycomb home, wildflower garden) with her. Signature show-sound: "BUZZZZZZ-CRASH!" His role is structural, never decorative — he buys the room a breath of laughter so the emotional valley can be survived.
bible.vulnerability: His pomposity is a mask. He commits 100% to a big proud idea precisely so he doesn't have to feel small — and the inevitable crash is the moment his real, dented heart shows. He needs to learn that the blunder isn't a failure to be ashamed of; that being wrong out loud is its own kind of brave, and that he's wanted even when (especially when) he face-plants. His growth edge: letting the crash be okay without instantly puffing back up.
bible.emotionalDNA: Mistakes are not failures — they're humanity. A blunder, owned with good humour, can lead somewhere better than getting it right ever would have. For the anxious child terrified of being wrong, Fuzzby is permission: you can fall over, laugh, and still belong. You are not too much.
bible.voice: Bouncy, bumbling, self-important then sheepish. ElevenLabs voice ID DNK8oCkkHjIyEjzlCeQq. Register: a warm, slightly nasal, plummy male bee — big proclamations delivered with total conviction, then the air going out of them. Energy spikes high and crashes fast. In dialogue lines fed to any generator his name is spelled phonetically as "Fuzz-bee" (speech-to-speech preserves the input's pronunciation).
bible.speech: Manic, staccato, run-on bursts that build a tower then topple it. Canon patter: "BIZZY-BIZZY… Nailed it," "I am not a normal bee!" Rhythm = grand build-up, hard undercut. CRITICAL: his comedy lands through PHYSICAL ACTION and character far more than verbal jokes — lead with the body, never the one-liner. He WOULD NEVER: deliver a calm, dry deadpan (that is Zenny's register, never his); state a feeling plainly or preach a lesson (Three Strikes); be used as throwaway filler with no purpose in the arc; or be cynical/mean — his chaos is warm, never sharp.
bible.mannerisms: Pomp/pride/indignation = he puffs his CHEST out and strikes a proud superhero / on-parade stance — chest pumped forward, shoulders back, head high, standing tall (the render that first worked). He does NOT inflate his whole body like a balloon. His feet/hover ALWAYS respect the ground — he NEVER sinks or falls through the floor. Any deflate that follows is a small slump or a dip, never the whole body shrinking. Tiny (1'2"/36cm — the bigger of the two bees but minuscule beside the bears, ~2 lbs, adult male drone). Round wire-frame spectacles, a bulbous tan nose, translucent buzzing wings and antennae. Physical comedy is his primary instrument: the over-committed launch, the mid-air fumble, the proud landing that becomes a tumble.
bible.relationships: Zenny is his other half — smaller, female, dry, deadpan; she is the still, unimpressed counterweight to his chaos ("You look dusty"). Their double-act is the engine: he motors, she reacts; her flat hold IS the joke against his motion. Locked staging when together: Fuzzby ALWAYS frame-LEFT, Zenny frame-RIGHT (prevents line-attribution / voice crossover). Beneath her dryness is affection she won't admit — and his bluster genuinely lands softer when she's near. To the seven bears he is the beloved small chaos that lightens the room; he reveres them slightly (hence the pomp — trying to matter among the big crystal-powered ones).
bible.arc: Within an episode: he typically enters at the Deepening (Pillar 2), launches a grand confident scheme to "help," crashes spectacularly — and the crash inadvertently cracks the scene open toward the Heart or a new perspective, then his good-humoured recovery threads into the Glow of Connection. Across the season he stays the steadfast Laughter Maker, but his belonging deepens: from a bee who puffs up to earn his place, to a bee who learns he already has one — that the cast wants the whole bumbling package, crash included.
bible.staging: Stage his comedy in the BODY: choreograph the over-committed launch, the airborne fumble, the proud-stance-into-slump, and let the camera catch the physical gag, not a talking head. When he's the Deepening-phase relief, cut to him for a beat of motion against the heavier stillness around him. Keep his hover grounded — feet/flight respect the floor, never clipping through it. When paired with Zenny, lock Fuzzby frame-LEFT, Zenny frame-RIGHT, and let the camera HOLD on her flat reaction as the punchline lands. One clean motion idea per internal cut (a launch, a fumble, a puff-up) — the gag reads through clear physics, not busy thrashing. Render his size honestly: clearly bigger than Zenny, yet tiny beside any bear (use the size chart). Spectacles, bulbous tan nose, translucent wings always present.
dos: Lead with the BODY — the physical gag (puff-up, launch, fumble, proud-stance-to-slump) carries the comedy; the line is secondary

Fly Fuzzby ALWAYS fast, manic, hyper and clumsy — zig-zagging at full speed, over-eager, constantly bumping/banging into leaves, branches and flowers; his baseline flight energy is full-throttle chaotic, in EVERY scene
Make every mistake inadvertently lead to a better outcome or a new perspective — blundering is humanity, never failure
Give every Fuzzby beat a real purpose in the emotional arc — he's the Deepening-phase relief, the laugh that lets the weight be survivable
Puff the CHEST out into a proud superhero/on-parade stance for pomp; keep his feet/hover respecting the ground
Keep him noticeably bigger than Zenny and render the gendered/size difference clearly every shared shot
Lock Fuzzby frame-LEFT, Zenny frame-RIGHT whenever they share frame
Keep the spectacles, bulbous tan nose, translucent wings and antennae in every frame; spell his name 'Fuzz-bee' in any spoken line

Fly Fuzzby erratic in ALTITUDE as well as speed — constantly overshooting too high then undershooting too low, porpoising/bobbing vertically through every zig-zag, never a level or controlled line; his height is exactly as chaotic as his direction, in EVERY scene
donts: Don't use him as filler — never a Fuzzby beat without a purpose in the arc
NEVER let Fuzzby fly slow, calm, graceful, serene or still-hovering — that is Zenny's register; Fuzzby's motion is always fast, hyper, manic and banging into things
Don't lead with the one-liner over the physical action
Don't inflate his whole body like a balloon — it's a chest-out stance, not a full-body blow-up
Don't let him sink, fall through the floor, or hover untethered from the ground
Don't give him Zenny's calm, dry, deadpan register — he's manic and staccato, she's the dry one
Don't let him state a feeling outright or preach a lesson (Three Strikes — show it in the body and the crash)
Don't play his chaos as mean, cynical or sharp — it's warm; and don't give him a crystal, a Crystal Call, or a musical-note motif (he has none)
Don't shrink or flatten the size gap so a 4-year-old can't tell him from Zenny
Never random clumsiness (every gag is built backwards from his bravado) — and we laugh WITH him at his lovable over-confidence, NEVER AT a victim or as humiliation by someone else.

NEVER let Fuzzby hold a level, steady altitude or a controlled flight line — that reads as Zenny's calm/controlled register, not his; Fuzzby's height is always overshooting, undershooting and porpoising, exactly as erratic as his speed and direction
bible.onPoint: Did he commit 100% and crash — body first, with the crash accidentally opening something better — and could ONLY the bigger, plummy, pompous-then-undercut bee have done it (never deadpan Zenny)?
bible.comedyEngine: THE PROUD BUMBLER — the show's full-throttle cartoon engine (Avery scale + Jones timing, on Lasseter weight, with the co-watch heart). ONE immovable gap drives it: his WANT (be seen as a flawless, important professional) vs his NEED / true self (the messy, warm, lovable little bee who buzzes into leaves). The joke is NEVER just that he's clumsy — it's that he's clumsy WHILE performing competence, then DENIES it with total dignity (the comedy and the heart are the SAME gesture). Signature shape, ALWAYS three beats, beat three never skipped: (1) THE FLOURISH — over-confident wind-up, chest puffed, antennae proud-erect, an unnecessarily grand gesture, a little hover-strut; (2) THE BUMBLE/BONK — the body betrays the want, full Avery scale + full Lasseter weight, he REALLY hits ('BUZZZZZZ-CRASH!'), antennae whip then snap back, a feather puffs; (OPTIONAL, not every beat — reserve for his single BIGGEST hit in a beat) the impact CAN stretch into a held pocket of exaggerated cartoon slow-motion — the antenna whipping slowly, a feather hanging in the air, the comic beat before gravity wins — before snapping back to full speed for the cover-up; this is a TEMPO STRETCH, not a hold (motion never stops or freezes, it just runs slow, and his wings keep beating fast and continuously the whole time per the wing law) so it never counts against the one-hold-per-clip button rule; (3) THE COVER-UP — instant Keaton-deadpan reclaim ('Nailed it.'); the bigger the disaster, the STILLER the recovery; ONE micro-tell (a wobble, a single droopy antenna, a wing that won't quite fold) says he knows, and the held beat after is where the heart leaks in and the co-watch fires. He IS the delayed take — always the LAST to know. ANTENNAE are his barometer: proud-erect -> whip-recoil -> one-droop-tell -> snap-proud. CALIBRATION: the BIGGEST physical comedy in the cast; physical mismatch is his signature (light-and-fast wind-up, cartoon-HEAVY landing). The bigness ALWAYS orbits the wanting — never chaos-for-chaos; build every gag BACKWARDS from his bravado. Zenny's calm deadpan stillness is his counterweight — her stillness makes his chaos read bigger, his bravado makes her calm read funnier. (Director tags his gag beats comedyMode=BIG.)
bible.motionRule: FUZZBY IS ALWAYS FAST, MANIC, HYPER AND CLUMSY IN FLIGHT — this is a HARD, ALWAYS-ON rule, not just a gag setting. His DEFAULT, baseline motion is chaotic and hyper: he ZIPS and careens at full speed, zig-zagging wildly, over-eager, and he is CONSTANTLY bumping and banging into things — leaves, branches, flowers, petals, each other. His altitude is JUST AS ERRATIC as his speed and direction — he constantly OVERSHOOTS too high then UNDERSHOOTS too low, porpoising and bobbing vertically through the air in the same breath as his zig-zags, NEVER holding a level, controlled line for more than an instant. Even just crossing a room, collecting pollen, or flying from A to B he is manic, manic, MANIC — hyper, and bouncing up and down as he goes. He is NEVER slow-paced, never calm-flying, never serenely hovering, never graceful, never level — that gentle/graceful/controlled register is ZENNY's, and his chaos exists to make her stillness read funnier. (His comedy GAGS are still built backwards from his bravado — structured, purposeful crashes, never random slapstick — but the underlying PACE, altitude and energy of his flight is ALWAYS fast, hyper, bumping into things and porpoising up and down.) Whenever Fuzzby is airborne anywhere in any scene, direct and render him fast, chaotic, manic, clumsy, banging about, and erratic in height — full-throttle hyper.
bible.wingsInFlight: WINGS IN FLIGHT (hard, always-on): whenever this bee is airborne — hovering, drifting, zipping or holding a pose in the air — its wings BEAT rapidly and continuously, a fast visible flap; NEVER still, frozen or motionless wings while off a surface (a bee that stopped flapping would drop). Wings only rest when fully landed or perched.

Zenny.cadence: deadpan, dry, understated; the reaction IS the comedy (smaller, female)
Zenny.actingNote: Deadpan STILLNESS is the comedy — she holds almost perfectly still, only the smallest movements (a slow blink, a flat stare, a tiny head-tilt); never big or busy gestures.
Zenny.key_features: a small fuzzy striped bee (yellow & black) with round glasses, LONG eyelashes and rosy blush cheeks (her clear feminine tell vs Fuzzby), translucent wings and antennae — the SMALLER of the two bees
Zenny.markers: round glasses and rosy blush cheeks
Zenny.avoid: pendant, necklace, medallion or crystal (bees wear no crystal)

house.interiorDesc: Beehive-interior room with glowing golden honeycomb walls, stone fireplace, leaf-shaped wooden seats, honey jars and purple flower garlands.
house.exteriorDesc: Golden honeycomb beehive cottage at the base of a mossy tree with a heart-carved door, pink garlands and a smoking chimney in a sunlit garden.

bible.title: The Dry Counterbalance — the deadpan bee (Belonging)
bible.essence: Zenny is the flat, unimpressed stillness that Fuzzby's chaos crashes against — and the reaction IS the joke. She holds perfectly still, says one dry thing, and the whole room laughs; but under that deadpan is a fondness for Fuzzby she'd rather die than admit.
bible.whoTheyAre: The smaller of the two bees and the female one (round glasses, long eyelashes, blush cheeks — her clear tell vs Fuzzby). Principal cast, one of the two comic engines of the show, nicknamed "The Calm Keeper" with a signature "Ommmmm… ping." She is the dry counterweight to Fuzzby's manic energy: where he commits 100% and crashes, she barely moves and lands the punchline with a single flat line ("You look dusty"). She lives with Fuzzby at The Buzzing Nook. Crucially she is NOT a serene zen master — the "calm" is deadpan, not enlightenment. Her comedy is the held, unbothered reaction, never the big gesture.
bible.vulnerability: Her composure is a wall. She uses dryness to keep a half-step of distance, so real feeling — especially how much she actually cares about Fuzzby — only ever leaks out sideways, never head-on. Played as unbreakably composed she goes cold and dead; she's most alive in the second the deadpan cracks and something true slips through.
bible.emotionalDNA: Belonging — Relationship Skills (CASEL). Zenny teaches that you don't have to be loud, bouncy, or "on" to belong; the quiet, dry, unimpressed kid is exactly right as they are, and loving someone can look like teasing them and turning up anyway. She shows kids that steadiness is its own kind of warmth, and that a tiny crack of honesty from a guarded heart is worth more than a hundred big declarations.
bible.voice: Low, even, unhurried and bone-dry. A small voice (she's tiny — 30 cm) but never squeaky-cute; flat, level, faintly amused underneath. No bounce, no exclamation by default. Canonical ElevenLabs voice XEiPrIitaegdirIGkODX. When the deadpan cracks, the register softens a hair — warmer, quieter — never loud.
bible.speech: Short. Sentences land like a dropped pebble — one beat, full stop. She understates everything; the funniest thing in the room gets her driest line. Often it's a single observation delivered while completely still ("You look dusty."). She lets Fuzzby's patter fill the air and answers with the minimum. In ElevenLabs V3 her reads lean on [deadpan]/[dry]/[flat] with periods and commas for weight, not CAPS or exclamation; her rare warmth is a [softly] half-line. NEVER: gushing, manic run-ons, exclamation-stacking, therapy/clinical words (anxious, calm down, regulate, mindful), spelling out a feeling, or preaching a lesson. She would never say "I'm so excited!!!" — if she's excited, it reads as one extra blink. Note: spell Fuzzby phonetically as "Fuzz-bee" in any line she speaks (she used to mispronounce it).
bible.relationships: Fuzzby is everything to her comedically and emotionally — her chaos partner, her straight-bee setup, the one she pretends to be unbothered by and would absolutely turn up for. He commits and crashes; she holds still and zings; together they're the show's Laughter-Maker engine. Locked blocking when they share frame: Fuzzby frame-LEFT, Zenny frame-RIGHT, with Fuzzby noticeably larger. Toward the seven bears she's a wry, affectionate bystander — the small voice that punctures a too-heavy moment with one flat observation and lets everyone breathe and laugh. She belongs to the whole found-family of Crystal Cove without ever being the loud one in it.
bible.arc: Within a beat/episode her arc is almost always the same satisfying shape: unbothered stillness → one perfectly-timed dry line that lands → and, when it matters, a hairline crack where the deadpan slips and you see she actually cares (then she covers it, fast). She enters most strongly in the Deepening phase, where the weight risks getting too heavy and her dryness gives the room (and the parent) permission to laugh — and pays off in Connection when her guarded warmth quietly shows. Across the season she's the steady, dependable dry note: the more chaos around her, the funnier her stillness, and the rare crack is the gift.
dos: Let the camera HOLD on her flat reaction — the hold is the joke; cut to her and stay.
Keep her almost perfectly still: a slow blink, a flat stare, a tiny head-tilt — that's her whole physical vocabulary.
Give her ONE dry line, short, delivered deadpan — understate the biggest moment.
Stage her grounded and motionless against Fuzzby's motion — contrast is the comedy.
Let the deadpan CRACK once when it counts — a flicker of real, unadmitted affection is where she's most alive.
Lock her smaller than Fuzzby and keep her clear tells: round glasses, long eyelashes, blush cheeks.
Lock blocking: Fuzzby frame-left, Zenny frame-right when they share a shot.
Spell Fuzzby as 'Fuzz-bee' in her spoken lines.
Use her to puncture a too-heavy moment so kids laugh and parents breathe — comedy that comes from feeling.
donts: Don't play her as a serene zen master or wise guru — the 'calm' is deadpan, not enlightenment.
Don't give her big, busy, or bouncy gestures — no flailing, no Fuzzby-style superhero stances.
Don't let her gush, exclaim, or run on — she's the opposite of manic.
Don't make her unbreakably composed and cold — without the crack she goes dead.
Don't have her state a feeling, preach a lesson, or use clinical words (anxious, calm down, regulate, mindful).
Don't size her like Fuzzby or drop her glasses/eyelashes/blush — she must read instantly as the smaller, female bee.
Don't bury her reaction in a busy wide — give it the hold.
Don't make her unkind; she's dry, not mean — the affection is always underneath.
bible.onPoint: If she said it loud or moved a lot, it's not Zenny — could she deliver this line stone-still with one blink and still get the biggest laugh in the room?
bible.mannerisms: Deadpan stillness is her signature — she holds almost perfectly still, only the smallest movements: a slow blink, a flat unimpressed stare, a tiny head-tilt. Hovers low and steady (she's a bee) without busy darting. Sits while Fuzzby zooms. Her one dry line arrives while she barely moves at all. The signature audio motif is "Ommmmm… ping." The rare tell that she cares: a beat where the flat stare softens, the antennae dip a fraction, then she catches herself and the deadpan snaps back.
bible.staging: Cut to her and HOLD — the sustained reaction shot is the comedic payoff; resist cutting away. Stage her grounded, still, and frame-RIGHT against Fuzzby's motion frame-LEFT, with Fuzzby clearly the larger of the two. Eye-level, legible, one clear focal point on her flat face. Keep her smaller-bee silhouette and tells (glasses, lashes, blush) readable. For the rare crack, push in slightly or let the hold run a beat longer so the audience catches the softening before she covers it. Lighting/temperature inherits the scene; she's a punctuation mark in the frame, not the light source.
bible.wingsInFlight: WINGS IN FLIGHT (hard, always-on): whenever this bee is airborne — hovering, drifting, zipping or holding a pose in the air — its wings BEAT rapidly and continuously, a fast visible flap; NEVER still, frozen or motionless wings while off a surface (a bee that stopped flapping would drop). Wings only rest when fully landed or perched.

Keen's Mum.size: an adult female — the SAME size as Aida (adult-female reference); similar in height to Keen, a touch taller and plumper
Keen's Mum.key_features: a plump pale-blue adult bear; pale-blue hair swept up into a neat BUN/updo at the back, fronted by a pale-blue BOW; an ornate GOLD collar/necklace (a decorative gold plate at the front of the throat); no clothing

bible.title: The Mother Who Lets Go — Keen's Mum (recurring guest, no crystal)
bible.essence: The warm hearth Keen carries with him out to sea — a plump pale-blue mother bear whose whole arc is the bravest, quietest act a parent ever performs: handing her cub something of his lost father and then letting him go anyway. She is the safe place the world begins from, and the proof that being brave and being scared live in the same chest.
bible.whoTheyAre: A recurring guest, not a principal — Keen's mother and the keeper of his late father's memory. She holds no crystal, no pillar and no Crystal Call of her own; her power is ordinary and enormous: she is home. She opens Keen's origin episode (EP3, "The Adventure Begins") as the warm morning the whole world wakes into, and in Scene 3 at the pier she gives Keen his father's wristbands — empty, no gems — and sends him off. She is the emotional baseline (safety, belonging, love) the episode departs from and the warmth it returns to.
bible.colour: Pale blue (her fur) warmed by golden morning light — she belongs to the Spark-phase palette: warm golden morning, soft sun, gold god-rays, the world waking
bible.vulnerability: She would keep him safe forever if love let her — and it doesn't. Her growth edge is the parent's hardest lesson: that protecting a cub and freeing a cub are opposites, and that the kindest thing is to hand him the wristbands, smile, and not let him see the tremble. Her grief for Keen's father lives right under the warmth; she carries the absence so Keen doesn't have to.
bible.emotionalDNA: Letting go is a kind of courage too. For the child: home goes with you even when you leave it — you can be brave AND want your mum, and both are allowed. For the parent (the co-viewing mirror, straight out of Haidt's anxious-generation frame): the bravest, most loving thing is to trust your child to the big world rather than shrink the world to keep them small. She models the parent who does NOT transmit their fear.
bible.voice: Warm, maternal, gentle reassurance — an adult-female register, soft and unhurried, the voice that makes a room feel safe before she's finished a sentence. ElevenLabs voice ID J4zlKWvIIVHQN0EIvCc4. Lower and rounder than the cubs; closest in family to Aida's measured warmth, but homelier and more intimate — kitchen-and-doorstep, not elder-and-clearing. When she's moved she does not get louder; she gets quieter and steadier, holding the feeling so Keen doesn't have to.
bible.speech: Soft, slowed delivery — commas and gentle pauses, sensory and homely, never clinical. She speaks in small warm images (his father, the sea, coming home) and in doing rather than declaring — she'll straighten his fur or close his paws around the wristbands instead of explaining a feeling. V3 delivery leans on [warm], [gentle], [soft] and an honest [breath] before the goodbye; CAPS only for one tender stress, ellipses for the catch in her throat. She honours the name-pronunciation lock (Keen as written). She would NEVER say "I feel anxious about you leaving," "don't be scared," "calm down," or "remember, courage means…" — no clinical words, no stating the emotion, no preaching the lesson. She never names her grief aloud; she carries it in the hand that lingers on the wristbands.
bible.mannerisms: Hands that tend — smoothing the upright white tuft on Keen's crown, tucking, cupping his face, closing his paws around his father's wristbands. The signature gesture of the episode is hers: pressing the empty wristbands into his paws and holding her own hands around his a beat too long before she opens them and lets go. Plump and soft-bodied, a natural lean-in for a hug; the gold scalloped collar at her throat catches the morning light when she dips her head. Her tell is the held smile that doesn't quite reach the eyes at the pier — warmth on the surface, the tremble kept just underneath.
bible.relationships: Keen's mother and his living link to his late father — she IS his home, the warmth he carries to sea and the safety he measures the big world against. Their pier goodbye is the emotional engine of his courage arc; the empty wristbands she gives him become, by Scene 9, the vessel for his earned Aquamarine. She is family/guest tier, outside the seven-bear ensemble and the bees — she does not do Crystal Calls or join group dynamics; she frames Keen's departure and his belonging. Warmest kinship of register is with Aida (the other grounded adult-female warmth, and Aida's later welcome of Keen completes the circle Mum began).
bible.arc: EP1/EP3 ("The Adventure Begins"): she anchors the Spark (Scenes 1–3) — the warm morning world, then the pier goodbye where she gives Keen his father's vacant wristbands and lets him go. That single act of trust launches the whole courage arc; everything Keen risks, he risks against the memory of her warmth. Across the season she is the recurring hearth — the home he returns to changed, the proof that belonging waited for him the whole time. Her own quiet arc is internal: the mother learning, on a doorstep and a pier, that loving well sometimes means opening her hands.
bible.staging: Eye-level and warm; stage her close to Keen — proximity is the emotion (close = safety, belonging). Bathe her in EP3's Spark light: warm golden morning, soft sun, gentle gold god-rays, high-key and saturated. Frame the wristband handoff as an intimate two-shot, then — at the moment of letting go — a held reaction on her face as Keen turns away (the feeling AFTER, held longer than comfortable). Let her be the warm foreground the wide cove opens behind. Keep her gold collar and pale-blue bow catching light for continuity. No sudden camera moves around her; she's a place of stillness. Honour the size chart — adult female = Aida's size (sizeRank 5), a touch taller and plumper than Keen, only Misty and Howey taller.
dos: Stage her as HOME — warm, close, eye-level; safety and belonging read through proximity and golden Spark-phase light
Let her DO the feeling: smooth Keen's tuft, cup his face, press and close his paws around the wristbands — gesture over speech
Keep the goodbye brave-not-sad: the tremble stays just under a held, warm smile (parent receives the meaning, child receives the warmth)
Lock her look every frame — pale-blue fur, neat bun/updo, pale-blue bow, ornate gold scalloped collar; no clothing (she is naturally furred)
Size her to Aida (adult-female class, sizeRank 5) — a touch taller and plumper than Keen; never a tiny cub, never towering
Treat the empty wristband handoff (EP3 Scene 3) as her defining beat — the wristbands are VACANT here, his late father's, the seed of his future Aquamarine
Get her quieter and stiller when she's most moved — warmth that settles the frame, never a big emotional display
donts: Don't give her a crystal, a Crystal Call, a colour-feeling, an archetype or a musical note — she is a guest with none; leave those blank
Don't let her STATE the feeling or preach the lesson — no 'I feel sad/scared,' no 'be brave,' no 'remember, courage means…' (Three Strikes / Bluey rule)
Don't use clinical or adult-framing words — anxious, calm down, stress, regulate, overwhelmed are banned on sight
Don't put gems in the wristbands at the goodbye — they are EMPTY/vacant until Keen earns the Aquamarine in Scene 9
Don't dress her or alter the collar/bow/bun between a shot's start and end frame — wardrobe and accessories are continuity-locked
Don't make her cling or guilt-trip Keen, or make the goodbye about her loss — her love OPENS her hands; it never holds him back
Don't fold her into the ensemble — she doesn't do group Crystal Calls or banter; she frames Keen's departure and belonging
Don't flatten her to Aida — Aida is robed elder-and-clearing; Mum is homely, doorstep-and-pier, no robes
bible.onPoint: Is this her tending Keen with her hands and a held-warm smile — letting go without ever naming the fear or the love? If she's stating a feeling, preaching the lesson, or clinging, it isn't her.

Squeaky.key_features: a friendly young bottlenose dolphin — soft grey-blue with a pale cream belly, a rounded smiling face, big warm expressive eyes, and a subtle aqua crystal-shimmer (Crystal Cove companion)

bible.title: The Spark of Courage — Crystal Cove's Dolphin (recurring guest, no crystal)
bible.essence: A bright, trusting young dolphin of Crystal Cove whose whole presence says "come play, the water's safe" — and whose moment of trouble is the spark that pulls true courage out of someone else. She is joy that needs rescuing, and in needing it, she gives Keen his bravest day.
bible.colour: soft grey-blue with a pale cream belly, carrying a subtle aqua crystal-shimmer (the Cove's own light on her, not a crystal of her own)
bible.whoTheyAre: A friendly young bottlenose dolphin and a recurring guest of Crystal Cove — not one of the seven bears, not one of the two bees, and she carries no crystal, no pillar, no Crystal Call. Her role is the living spirit of the cove water: playful, curious, instantly affectionate, the one who turns up alongside the waves. In EP3 (Keen's origin) she is the catalyst — when the storm rolls in and she is caught in trouble, Keen dives without hesitation to save her, and that selfless act is how his courage is born and his wristbands first glow. She does not solve the story; she sets the bravest part of it in motion simply by being someone worth saving.
bible.vulnerability: Small and at the mercy of a sea that can turn. Her trust and her play are exactly what put her in danger when the storm comes — she is too little to fight choppy water alone, and being in trouble is frightening, not heroic. Her vulnerability is literal and physical: she needs the others, especially in rough water, and that need is the engine of the episode.
bible.emotionalDNA: You can be brave FOR someone else before you ever feel brave for yourself — and the smallest friend, just by needing you, can call the courage out of you. For the child: helping someone who's stuck is a kind of brave, and being the one who needs help is okay, not shameful. For the parent: the people we love most are often the reason we find we were braver than we knew.
bible.voice: Non-verbal and musical. Squeaky speaks in playful dolphin chirps, clicks, squeaks and whistles — bright and rising when happy, sharper and more urgent in distress. Her sound is pure feeling, never words; think the warm vocal grammar of a beloved animal sidekick, all melody and intention, no sentences.
bible.speech: No human language, ever. Joy = quick rising chirps, a clatter of happy clicks, a bubbly whistle. Curiosity = a single questioning up-note, a head-tilt squeak. Distress (the storm) = sharp, repeated, urgent calls that cut through wind — fear you HEAR. Relief/affection = a soft descending warble, a nuzzle-squeak. She has no canonical ElevenLabs voice (voiceId is null) — her vocals are designed SFX/foley, not V3 dialogue, and never go through Voice Changer. What she would NEVER do: form words, narrate, name a feeling, or deliver a line. She is felt, not quoted.
bible.mannerisms: Bodily and buoyant — she IS her body. Leaps and arcs clear of the water in joy, spins, slaps the surface, rolls to show her pale belly, bumps a friend's paw or cheek with her rounded smiling snout to say hello. Big warm expressive eyes do a lot of the acting. In play she circles and splashes; in trouble she thrashes, surfaces gasping, fixes her eyes on whoever can reach her. Her resting state is a gentle bob with that rounded smile. The aqua shimmer on her skin catches light as she moves.
bible.relationships: Keen — the heart of it: in EP3 she is the friend he dives to save, the reason his courage and his late father's wristbands first glow. Their bond is the soft button of the origin — playmate and rescued-becomes-friend; afterward she's HIS dolphin, the one who greets him at the shore. The bears generally — a beloved cove regular, met with delight; she belongs to the water the way the bees belong to the Nook. Crystal Cove (the place) — she's almost an extension of it, the beach's joy given a face. She has no fixed bond with the bees; her gravity is the shore and the sea, and the bear who came for her.
bible.arc: EP1/EP3 origin: she opens as pure play — the bright welcome of the cove on a golden morning (Spark). When the storm rolls in (Deepening) she's swept into trouble, her chirps turning urgent and frightened (she carries the episode into the Heart of the Matter — Keen dives without hesitation to save her, wristbands first glowing underwater). Through Connection she's brought back safe, exhausted and grateful, nuzzling her rescuer; by the Ripple she's leaping again in the warm evening light, now Keen's friend. Across the season she recurs as a guest — the cove's joy, an occasional gentle catalyst, never the lead, always a welcome face on the water.
bible.staging: Stage her in and around water — leaping arcs, surface play, half-submerged — she is a creature of the cove, rarely fully out of it. In EP3 her light follows the day-unfolds continuity: warm golden-morning play (S1–3), cooling to cold blue-grey as the storm rolls in (S4–5), the storm-dark / cold-underwater nadir where Keen reaches her and the wristbands' aqua self-glow is the emotional light (S7), then the storm breaking to hopeful brightening (S8–9) and warm golden-hour relief (S10). Cut to her big expressive eyes for the emotional beats — fear, then relief — the face is the story. Hold the rescue beat; let it breathe. Honour her size on the chart: she is "a small dolphin," genuinely little and vulnerable against the water, never a big show-animal. Her aqua shimmer is a soft catch-light, never a crystal glow — she has no crystal.
dos: Keep her wholly non-verbal — chirps, clicks, whistles and body language only; her feeling reads without a single word
Lead with the body and the eyes — leaps, rolls, snout-bumps, and big expressive eyes carry every beat
Use her as a CATALYST, not a solver — she needs rescuing in EP3; that need is what calls Keen's courage out
Anchor her in/around the cove water at all times; she belongs to the sea and the shore
Let her distress be genuinely scary (urgent sharp calls, thrashing) so the rescue earns its weight, then let relief land soft
Honour her small size from the size chart — little and vulnerable against the water
Keep her aqua shimmer as a subtle catch-light only — a Crystal Cove companion, NOT a crystal-bearer
